The prophet laments that he lived among a people quickly moving toward ruin, in which many good people would suffer. People had no comfort, no satisfaction in their own families or in their closest relationships. Disregard and violation of family duties are a sad sign of widespread corruption. Those who are not respectful to their parents are unlikely to prosper. The prophet saw no safety or comfort except by looking to the Lord and waiting on God for his salvation. When facing trials, we should continually look to our Divine Redeemer, so that we may have strength and grace to trust in him, and be examples to those around us.

Those who are truly sorry for their sins will see good reason to be patient under suffering. When we complain to the Lord about the badness of the times, we should also complain against ourselves for the badness of our hearts. We must depend on God to work deliverance for us in due time. We must not only look to him, but expect him. In our greatest distresses, we will see no reason to despair of salvation if by faith we look to the Lord as the God of our salvation. Even though enemies triumph and insult, they will be silenced and put to shame. Even though Zion’s walls may remain in ruins for a long time, there will come a day when they shall be rebuilt. Israel will come from all distant places, not turning back because of discouragement. Even though our enemies seem to prevail against us and rejoice over us, we should not lose hope. Even though we are knocked down, we are not destroyed; we may join hope in God’s mercy with submission to his correction. No obstacles can prevent the blessings the Lord intends for his church.

When God is about to deliver his people, he encourages their friends to pray for them. Apply this prayer spiritually to Christ, asking him to care for his church as the great shepherd of the sheep, and to go before them while they are here in this world, which is like a wilderness. God promises that in answer to this prayer, he will do for them what repeats the miracles of former ages. Just as their sin brought them into bondage, so God’s forgiving their sin brought them out. All who find pardoning mercy cannot help but wonder at that mercy; we have reason to be amazed if we know what it is. When the Lord takes away the guilt of sin, so that it does not condemn us, he will break the power of sin, so that it does not control us. If left to ourselves, our sins will be too strong for us; but God’s grace will be enough to subdue them, so that they do not rule over us, and then they will not ruin us. When God forgives sin, he makes sure it never is remembered against the sinner again. He casts their sins into the sea—not near the shore where they may appear again, but into the depths of the sea, never to rise again. All their sins will be cast there, because when God forgives sin, he forgives all. He will complete what concerns us, and with this good work he will do everything for us that our situation requires, and that he has promised. These promises relate to Christ, and the success of the gospel until the end of time, the future restoration of Israel, and the final triumph of true religion in all lands. The Lord will fulfill his truth and mercy; not one jot or tittle of it will fail: He is faithful who has promised, and he also will do it. Let us remember that the Lord has given assurance of his covenant, for great comfort to all who flee for refuge to lay hold on the hope set before them in Christ Jesus.
